sentences.text: In September, end of September, 1944, when the Russians were about 70 miles from Cracow--
Yes.
As a matter of fact, we were lying down on the ground and hearing the, the, the shells falling, the, the artillery.
Which artillery, I don't know if it was the German or the Russian.
We preferred to, to-- we preferred to know-- to, to think that it was the Russian artillery, pounding the Germans.
On the guards?
No.
They just ignored it.
But at that time, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as, as I said before, we got on the list, on the Schindler's List, through the intercession of Raimund Titsch.
This was a great achievement of my father's.
[INAUDIBLE].
And we were herded into box cars, into cattle cars, right at the, at the Plazsow terminal.
It was a rail, rail terminal in Plazsow.
And we were taken from there, through Auschwitz.
We were a few hours in Auschwitz.
Because it was on-- in transition.
On the train, we were about 12 hours.
We didn't see too much of it.
But we, we saw the crematoria.
We saw the barracks of Auschwitz.
We saw the guards.
We saw the people being chased and so on.
Yes.
We knew that Auschwitz was a extermination camp.
By word of mouth, in the year 1943, '44.
We believed that this was, this was an extermination camp, no question.
No, no.
Because at that time already, the information got around in such a way that even Poles knew.
It really came from the Poles, the information.
Because their folk was also put into Auschwitz, for, for political reasons of whatever.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] From Auschwitz--
No, no.
We were-- but we were in Auschwitz.
The train took off.
We knew that we were going to the Schindler's factory.
Where exactly, I didn't know.
It meant for me that there was some ray of hope.
Because Schindler was known to be a protector of the Jews at that time already.
No.
He played his hands very carefully and very smartly, Schindler.
Otherwise, he would be put to death.
There's no question.
But he knew where to bribe, when to bride, how to gain favors and so on.
He knew exactly.
He-- in my opinion, in my belief that he was given a divine guidance in this, that he himself should not be killed.
And, consequently, the Jews should be saved.
No, but I knew that it exists.
It existed.
From there, we-- from Auschwitz, we were transported to Gross-Rosen, which was the main concentration camp, near Breslow.
We were there for two days, three days.
It was a camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where people were exterminated too.
And atrocities were committed in that camp.
There's no question.
We saw people standing for 12 hours in one position.
It was called the [NON-ENGLISH] commando.
Just standing, because there was no work for them or-- just for, you know, for-- to, to, to-- for ordeal's sakes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Without opening-- without moving, even.
There was any movement, there was immediately a beating, for any movement during the 12 hours standing in the [NON-ENGLISH] commando.
And we heard different things from, from, from inmates there.
That life expectancy was almost nil.
Terrible.
They looked like shadows, the people there, like shadows.
No, no.
But we knew that we were in transit.
Because this main camp had a branch, a work branch in Brunnlitz, where the Schindler's factory was about to be erected.
Just waited.
Waited and, and we had to run for food.
When we got a, a bowl of soup, we had to run and eat.
Eat it and-- on the double.
Eat it on the double.
Otherwise, the guards-- the, the Polish or Ukrainian guards-- they were, they were beating mercilessly people.
Once I got, I got a beating there.
At a Gross-Rosen.
Because I, I did not put my bowl in the right position, to the soup to be poured in.
So he gave me a beating for it, over the head.
Not in Plazsow.
In Plazsow, I got kicked by a German, when I didn't-- I didn't take off my hat soon enough.
So he called me over, and he kicked me in my genitals.
In Plazsow,
When you saw a German, immediately.
But you see, they always thought that you didn't take this-- the hat off, the hat off soon enough.
So he kicked me, in a very, very painful manner.
I suffered from it very much, for, for days later on.
Some people knew, yes.
So, lucky duck.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was not a, a sense of-- if you think that there was jealousy, no.
There was no jealousy.
It was just, I would say, condescension.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fine.
You got this, you're lucky.
Good for you.
No jealousy or you are-- oh, I would-- why not me or something.
Everybody was trying.
Everybody who, who-- everybody knew, wanted to get on the list.
Because they knew that there's some hope.
On the list is some hope, because of Schindler, of Schindler.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So from Gross-Rosen, we were again put on cattle cars and gone.
And we went to Brunnlitz, which was Sudetenland, really.
This was a-- this was German territory from 1938.
You know, they-- Hitler occupied the Sudetenland in 1938.
It was Czechoslovakia really.
But later on it, became Sudetenland, a part of Germany.
I think a piece of bread, that's it.
Yeah, only a piece of bread, that's all.
It was terrible, because there was, there was nowhere to sit in the train.
There was-- everybody sat, sat in one's lap on the train.
And it was atrocious, atrocious, atrocious.
People were vomiting into-- onto one another and were defecating on one another.
They were urinating into one another.
It was, it was just terrible.
Because the German was in the middle, with a gun.
And any approach, he was, he was immediately pointing the gun.
In the car with us.
A German, an SS man.
He was, he was sitting there in the middle.
And if somebody wanted to, to, to move closer, towards him or by inadvertently, he immediately pointed the gun, and everybody was squeezing back.
There was no name.
I don't know.
It was a young German, a young German.
I know that was a young German.
No.
People were sick, sick, mentally and physically sick when we get-- when you got off the train.
Because it was a mental anguish.
Terrible.
You know, to, to, to sit in one's lap and, and, and to see how people do their-- you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] their, their, their human needs upon one another.
It was terrible.
It was not only physical anguish but mental anguish too, yes.
No, no, no.
But my father was very distraught.
He was crying and, and, and very distraught, M father especially.
Because it was so unbearable, so degrading, so, so, so-- you can't imagine.
When we came to Brunnlitz--
I some-- I somehow bore the, the suffering better than my father.
I was younger.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to Brunnlitz.
We were put on, on a, a-- I don't know how you call it.
I forgot how you call it.
Beds one on top of the other.
Like bunks.
Three-- one, two, and three.
Very narrow, two to a bunk, very narrow.
There was not-- there was no room to, to, to turn even.
And there was straw.
Instead of mattresses, there was straw on the, on the, on the bunks.
Food was even scarcer than anywhere else.
There was a real starvation at Brunnlitz, a real starvation.
[SIGHS] We had once a day, in the, in the, in the evening, soup.
And in the morning, a small piece of bread, about 20 grams, which is a very small piece of bread.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the conscience that you don't have enough food makes you even more hungry.
So the starvation was widespread.
And, and the, the main topic of any conversation between two people was food.
Food.
This was the main topic.
Because by talking food, by speaking, by reminding ourselves, by remembering food, it, it, it, it was like a pleasure.
It was a spiritual pleasure to speak of food.
Of-- challah, cakes, good meat, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] good fish, Shabbas food, cholent.
This was, this was, this was a dream.
This was a dream.
Because you can't imagine what starvation does to people.
It animalizes people, starvation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't say that we were animals.
But instead of thinking of salvation, of liberation, of future, all we could think of and speak of and dream of was food.
Food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So in this itself, this has a certain percentage of animalization of a person.
Of course, we did not give up the faith in Hashem, the faith in God.
Those who, who had the background.
On the contrary, somebody-- I think Schindler, it was Schindler who smuggled [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] the head [NON-ENGLISH], the head phylactery into the camp.
And every morning, there was a, there was a line of people, standing to put on one [NON-ENGLISH] and say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Next, next, next.
In this starvation, there was Pesach, in April '45.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were holy people in the camp who did not want to eat this piece of little-- little piece of bread that we got every morning.

Because they wanted-- I mean, they were-- they, they, they had a, a sense of commitment, such deep commitment, to the Jewish way of life, to the Jewish religion and to Hashem, To God's-- to Torah, that they had foregone the little piece of bread.
And they ate roots, grass.
All through the Pesach.
I ate bread.
Because the Rabbis told me, you need to eat as, as much as you can and whatever you can .
Because this is [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] This is something that might save your soul.
It might save your body.
It was very hard to eat bread on Pesach.
I never ate bread, never ate before bread on Pesach.
But I did eat.
As a commitment to Torah, as a show of commitment to Torah, I preferred not to-- on the Seder night not to eat anything.
I fasted on Seder night, just to, to show that when I do eat bread the next morning, it will be because I have to.
Because I-- otherwise I'll, I'll, I'll expire from hunger.
Everybody knew the dates.
The dates were kept.
Dates were kept.
There was people of-- devout people, who knew the dates exactly and told it to everyone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody knew the dates.
Yes.
I saw him every day.
I saw Schindler every day.
[SIGHS] I described him many times already, as a man of tremendous, overwhelming personality.
He was always in a white shirt, chain smoking.
But he never finished the cigarette.
He always took a few puffs and threw the cigarette away, so that one of the inmates, prisoners, would be able to pick up and continue.
Because people were-- there were some people who were addicted to cigarettes-- addicted, addicted, who didn't care for food, but the cigarette was, was the life sustaining element for them.
Negative things?
In-- at--
In Brunnlitz?
Well, I was first of all, from malnutrition, I got sick.
I got boils over, all over my body.
And there was not enough, not enough medications.
So I was suffering all the time.
Not only hunger but pain, from, from, from wounds that I had on my body, from malnutrition.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] occurrence, when Raimund Titsch came to Brunnlitz.
And he saw us, and he saw my father.
And through his intercession, we got half of a bread.
This was-- we got, between me and my father, half of a loaf of bread.
This was the greatest, the greatest holiday for us.
To be able to eat a quarter, each one a quarter of a bread, of a loaf of a bread was-- not that we were satiated.
But there was some relief from starvation at that time, for, for, for a few hours.
In Brunnlitz, first of all, the, the factory, the munitions factory, was being built when we, when we came.
So I was schlepping machinery, presses, all kinds of machinery, schlepping, hauling, heaving, yes?
But towards the end, when the machinery was erected and started working-- it was in 1945 already, February-- I was transferred to, to, to painting of artillery shells, so they shouldn't get rusted.
So I painted them, with a, with a certain-- with a like a pistol, paint pistol.
So this was my, my job.
Who were there?
There were a few, quite a few people who I knew.
There were, first of all, Feigenbaum, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brothers Feigenbaum.
They had a, a-- on 47th Street in New York a diamond business, a very big diamond business.
Right now, they have still.
So they were with me.
I remember them.
There were very-- we were very befriended with them.
And also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was in Brunnlitz with me, Rosners, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the brothers Rosners.
They were the musicians, you see?
Again, somebody smuggled an accordion into the Brunnlitz camp.
I think the Czech, the Czech underground.
There was a Czech underground.
They smuggled a accordion [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] into the camp, into the Brunnlitz camp.
And one of the Rosners played, when the Russian-- the final Russian offensive started, on January 12, 1945.
This was the final offensive that brought the Russians into, into Berlin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] into outskirts of Berlin.
So he, he played, as a sign of happiness, a sign of hope that salvation is coming, that liberation is coming.
Yes.
Poldek-- Pfefferberg, His name was Pfefferberg, now it's Page.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was a man of great stature and brilliance.
He was an engineer.
He was very much in close contact and in close relationship with Schindler.
He helped Schindler in different-- I think in bookkeeping or something.
He helped Schindler in many, in many ways.
I saw when I was in camp.
I remember him.
He was a very handsome and very dark haired man of great verve and, and, and flair.
But now he's an old, an elderly man, of course.
But I remember him very well.
I remember, of course, Stern, the one that you saw on the movie.
Itzchok Stern.
He was a wonderful gentleman, a wonderful gentleman.
He especially cared for the younger people, for the teenagers like myself in the camp.
So he tried to, to, to give us some roots, you know, roots, should, should be able to, to, to still our hunger a little bit.
Very seldom that, that we, we, we were able to, to depend on his magnanimity in this.
Because there was-- there were many teenagers.
And only-- he could do only so much.
No, not in Brunnlitz.
No.
The women were completely separated from men.
Complete separation at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, everybody was thinking of food, of food and surviving the war.
This was the main preoccupation.
I believe I somehow wiggled out of it.
I did some acrobatic arrangements, not to have to work on Shabbas.
I think so.
Because I know exactly that in all the years of my ordeal of the Holocaust, I did everything possible to maintain and keep Shabbas.
Everything possible, short of endangering my life, which is, of course, the Torah does not want to endanger-- to, to-- one's life to be endangered by keeping Shabbas.
This is-- this would be going against the precepts of the Torah.
Yes.
I was in close contact with rabbis and very, very religious Jews in camp.
And again, they gave me lots, lots of encouragement, hope, foresight, knowledge.
They taught me what ever I, I, I, I could absorb.
Well, that was one rabbi.
I don't, I don't know his name.
But he was especially fond of me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I think his name was Perlman, Perlman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes, he was very fond of me.

And tried to give me courage, and, and, to, to, to wake up my spirit and to, to, to give me-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to give me not only [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] education-- education, but to give me some insight in what's going on and what-- the overview of the whole situation.
Not only the immediate needs and the immediate preoccupation, the immediate concerns, but the, the, the overview, the, the, the entire, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the entire-- how can I express it?
The situation in general, the generalities of the situation, not just the particulars.
Do you remember Schindler's wife?
Yes.
As a matter of fact, I met her here.
She was a very pretty woman, very, at that time, a very pretty woman.
Everybody admired her, as a, as a doll.
Because nobody was thinking of a woman at that time.
But a doll, you know, she was a nice doll walking around, together with him most of the time.
I think she did help out--
By myself, I, I was not a recipient of her help in any way.
But I know that she, she did help out in, in the camp.
At the very end-- I think it was in March '45-- there was a transport of emaciated Hungarian Jews that came into the camp.
And they didn't come into the camp, really.
They were in, in, in cattle cars.
And then they remained in the cattle cars, right at the, at the riding terminal of the camp.
I saw them.
They were living skeletons, living skeletons.
We couldn't do anything for them.
But I think Schindler put himself out, really, to help feed them, in, in, in the best way he could.
But there were-- I'm sure that there were many deaths, right there and there.
Because the, the people were just at the end of their tethers.
